name: pestle saag curry
instructions:
PREP THE VEGETABLES (BOTH DONE SEPARATELY BEFORE THE MASALA):
- POTATOES — PARBOIL: cube potatoes and parboil in salted water until just-tender-but-still-firm (a knife slides in with resistance). Drain and set aside. Parboiled potatoes hold their shape when they hit the masala and don't turn to mush.
- CAULIFLOWER — CRISPY-BAKED: toss cauliflower florets in vegetable oil, salt, and a little turmeric. Roast at 425°F until deeply browned and crispy at the edges. Set aside.

PESTLE THE SPINACH: pound fresh spinach (or mixed greens) in a mortar and pestle to a rough paste — not smooth, you want recognizable spinach pieces. Set aside.

BUILD THE MASALA: sauté finely chopped red onions in vegetable oil until softened. Add garlic and cook briefly. Add ground spices — cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, cardamom, chili powder, amchur powder — and toast in the oil until fragrant.

ADD THE CHEESE (REQUIRED — SEE NOTES):
- FRIED PANEER (canonical): cube paneer and shallow-fry in oil until golden on all sides. Add to the masala.
- OR MELTED SOFT WHITE CHEESE: monterrey jack or fontina, cubed or shredded, added later so it melts into the sauce without frying first.

COMBINE: fold the pestled spinach, parboiled potatoes, and crispy cauliflower into the masala. Warm through gently — don't over-cook, the vegetables are already prepped. Finish with a generous squeeze of lime.
notes:
Mortar-and-pestle approach to saag curry, as opposed to the blender-and-coconut version. I prefer this approach for two reasons:
- More spinach per bite. The pestle leaves recognizable spinach pieces in the dish rather than dissolving everything into a smooth sauce, so each bite has real spinach character.
- Better with structural vegetables. Potatoes and cauliflower don't want to be drowned in a blended coconut sauce — they get lost in it. The pestled version lets the spinach coat the vegetables without erasing them.

POTATOES ARE BEST PARBOILED (NOT BAKED, NOT SAUTÉED FROM RAW): parboil the cubed potatoes in salted water until just-tender-but-still-firm before folding them into the masala. Parboiled potatoes hold their shape when they hit the sauce and read as distinct chunks in each bite. Baked potatoes (the previous rule) come out too dry — they don't pick up the masala moisture. Sautéed-from-raw potatoes don't cook through in the time the masala needs. Parboiling is the middle path that lands the texture right.

CAULIFLOWER IS BEST CRISPY-BAKED BEFORE INTEGRATION: toss florets in oil + salt + a pinch of turmeric, roast hard at 425°F until browned and crispy at the edges, THEN fold into the finished masala. Steamed or raw-added cauliflower goes limp and loses its identity in the sauce. The pre-roasted crispy exterior holds up against the wet masala and each bite has textural contrast — the goal is discrete crunchy florets in the middle of the saag, not soft florets swimming in it.

CHEESE IS REQUIRED — NOT OPTIONAL: alongside indian spices, onion, spinach, and lime, the dish needs a cheese element to feel complete. The dairy fat rounds the spinach and spice register in a way that nothing else can. Options:
- FRIED PANEER: canonical, most traditionally indian. Cube and shallow-fry until golden before adding.
- MELTED SOFT WHITE CHEESE (MONTERREY JACK OR FONTINA): a real alternative, especially if you don't have paneer. Both melt smoothly into the sauce and carry the same dairy-fat role without the fried-paneer chew. This is non-traditional but works — the smooth-melty character gives the saag a slightly gratin-adjacent feel that's genuinely good, if different.
Don't skip the cheese entirely; the dish reads flat without it.

PROTEIN / VEGETABLE FORK: with cheese required, the fork is now between the cheese options above rather than paneer-vs-vegetables. Potatoes and cauliflower are the structural vegetables — treat them both as canonical additions, not either/or.

LIME IS REQUIRED — NON-NEGOTIABLE: reiterating this because it's easy to skip and the dish falls apart without it. Alongside cheese and the pestled spinach, the lime finish is what makes this recipe cohere. The brightness cuts the dairy fat and the earthy indian spices, and specifically LIME (not lemon) is the right acid — lime's more citrus-forward, slightly floral character reads right against the saag; lemon's rounder acid pushes the dish toward a lemon-butter register that doesn't work here. Squeeze fresh lime generously at the finish.

AJWAIN IS PART OF THE SPICE STACK: added ajwain to the primary spice list. Ajwain (carom seeds) has a thyme-and-oregano-adjacent character with a slight bitter bite that lands well in a saag — it pairs particularly well with paneer and with potato/cauliflower. Toast briefly with the cumin and coriander at the masala stage.

GREAT IN TORTILLAS / THIN WRAPS / QUESADILLA STRUCTURES: this curry works exceptionally well as a wrap or quesadilla filling. The cheese-plus-vegetables composition already reads gratin-adjacent, so folding it into a flour tortilla or thin flatbread and pan-crisping the outside (or stuffing between two tortillas quesadilla-style with more cheese and toasting) gives you a portable, structured version that leverages the same DNA. The lime and chili powder read especially well in this format — the acid and heat cut the melted-cheese richness on the crisp tortilla edge. If making tortillas from scratch for this, see the flour tortillas recipe.

TECHNIQUE NOTES:
- Mortar + pestle the spinach. Don't blender.
- Chili powder is necessary — don't skip it.
- No sunflower or pumpkin seeds — they pull this toward a pesto-adjacent dish, which isn't what we want.
